Each new skill that we go over is meant to build upon the others. It challenge you to practice tactical empathy in your daily life and to think of these skills as extensions of natural human interaction rather than artificial conversation texts. Try working them into your daily conversations and you'll master the initial awkwardness. Everyone wants to feel like they are being heard and understood. Tactical empathy then is just a way to practice acknowledging the other person and make them feel heard. No matter the situation, whether you're negotiating a multi-million dollar business deal or visiting with a neighbor, developing an empathic relationship and encouraging your counterpart to elaborate on what they're saying is the basis of healthy human interactions. Think of these as emotional best practices that help you navigate the inescapable awkwardness present in so many of our conversations. You'll find that as you practice your relationships and connections will become more meaningful. After all, the first step of negotiation is to build a foundation of trust and connect with your counterpart. As we wrap up this course, keep in mind the following lessons we went over. Try seeing it from their perspective, which is observing it, not feeling it by putting yourself in their shoes. You don't know where those funky shoes have been anyway. Empathy isn't about agreeing with someone else's ideas, but if you acknowledge their situation you'll convey that you're paying attention. Once they know you're listening, they will trust you and be more likely to reveal something you can use. Clear off into reasons why your counterpart will not make an agreement with you is more powerful than why they will make a deal. Clear the barriers first in order to reach an agreement. If you don't tackle the negative influences head on, you're doing a huge disservice to both you and your counterpart. Using labels is an incredibly easy way to diminish these negative influences and bring them to light. The faster you can interrupt the amygdala's reaction to a situation, the faster you can diffuse the fear in your counterpart and work towards a foundation of trust-based influence. After you throw out a label or a mirror, make sure you stop talking and pause. The silence will help your statement to sink in and do its work properly. Another great way to deal with negatives ahead of time is to list out all the worst things that the other party could possibly say about you. Performing an accusations audit heads off negative dynamics even before they take root. Because these accusations often sound exaggerated when said a lot of putting them out there will encourage the other person to claim the opposite is true. Labels can also be used to reassure a person who is looking to be appreciated and understood. Remember, as your rule of thumb, labeling negatives diffuses them while labeling positives reinforces them.
